title: Industry Pro Expert: Doro Rojas on Wood Flooring, Finishes, and Installation
summary: Erin Stetzer hosted Doro Rojas of Doro's Unique Flooring, a 28-year wood flooring veteran running a team of 80-95 people, for an expert Q&A session. The conversation covered the differences between UV-cured and oxidation finishes, engineered vs. solid hardwood, proper climatization, sequencing wood floor installation within a build, and specialty topics like brass inlay, chevron installation, radiant heat, and custom patterns. Participants from across the country asked detailed technical questions and received both product recommendations and trade references.

teachings.explanation: UV-cured finishes are run through a machine with UV lights that force the finish to cure rapidly, resulting in a look many clients perceive as plastic or artificial. Oxidation finishes are allowed to cure naturally over 8-12 hours or more, soaking into the wood and producing a more natural, authentic appearance. The oxidation process can be water-based or oil-based and is what Doro almost exclusively uses.
teachings.quote: The uv cure you have a finish line that you go right through it and by the time you get it at the other end that wood is ready to be installed... but the one thing that a lot of customers they pick up right away when they come to the showroom is okay i don't like that one i don't like that one i like this one and they kept coming back to the oxidation finish because they don't look plastic.

teachings.explanation: A poly finish (water-based or oil-based polyurethane) builds a coating on top of the wood, creating a surface that can look plastic. An oil/oxidation finish is burnished into the wood, requiring 6-8 steps but resulting in a more natural feel and look with no surface crust. The oil finish runs approximately 20% more in cost due to additional labor and time.
teachings.quote: When it comes to like a poly floor uh you raise the green one coat of stain and two coats of poly and you're finished and done versus the oil finish which we do a lot sometimes to achieve that finish it takes up to six or eight steps.

teachings.explanation: Wood floors must be climatized to the environment where they will be installed before installation begins. Doro recommends at minimum two weeks of climatization and always checks moisture content against NWFA guidelines by region. Failure to climatize causes cupping, gapping, and floor movement. Temporary HVAC units should be run at the permanent living temperature of the home.
teachings.quote: I would not install a floor if the house is not acclimated.

teachings.explanation: The National Wood Flooring Association (NWFA) publishes regional moisture content maps that specify what moisture reading wood should have before installation. For example, Houston requires 9-12% moisture content, while Aspen requires around 6%. Installers must test wood moisture with a meter and match it to the regional specification before proceeding.
teachings.quote: Go to nwfa and you're gonna see like a little map and it's gonna tell you okay this floor needs to be reading at this moisture content.

teachings.concept: Shoe Mold as a Coverup, Not a Feature
teachings.explanation: Shoe mold exists primarily to conceal imperfect installation where the floor meets the wall or baseboard. Doro's preference is to avoid shoe mold entirely and instead achieve a clean finish where baseboards sit directly on top of finished flooring. When shoe mold is required or desired, custom profiles can be milled rather than using standard half-inch by three-quarter-inch round profiles.
teachings.quote: The rule of shuma... shuma is to cover any imperfection of an installer that's what it is.

teachings.explanation: When storing wood on-site prior to installation, boards should be sticker stacked, meaning alternating layers are placed perpendicular to each other to allow airflow through the entire stack. Simply piling boards flat on top of each other does not allow proper air circulation and the wood will not acclimate evenly.
teachings.quote: You put one like this and then the other one the opposite way because you need that airflow to go right through the whole entire flooring.

teachings.concept: Floor Sequencing and Transition Point Strategy
teachings.explanation: On large homes, wood floor installation can be broken into zones and worked in phases, but only if natural transition points are used as stopping points. Stopping mid-room makes blending nearly impossible. The finishing crew should use those transition points, then polish to blend any visible differences when returning for the final coat. The final coat should happen as close to move-in as possible.
teachings.quote: The best advice that i can tell you to tell your installer or your finisher is let's find a good transition so when you go back to the final it's gonna blend nice and even.

teachings.concept: Yellowing Prevention with Water-Based Finishes and Tannin Neutralization
teachings.explanation: All wood floors will amber or yellow over time to some degree. The best way to slow this is to neutralize the tannic acids in the wood and use a water-based finish product rather than oil-based. Darker stain colors will show yellowing more than natural tones. UV cure does not help prevent yellowing.
teachings.quote: How do you prevent a floor from amber or to turn yellow is how you neutralize everything that you do... the best product for that is to stick to a water base and to neutralize all the acids on the wood the tannic acids.

teachings.concept: Pre-Finished Oxidation in a Shop Setting
teachings.explanation: To avoid the time and logistical challenges of on-site finishing, Doro developed a system of applying oxidation finishes in his warehouse on carts before shipping to job sites. This preserves the quality of the oxidation finish while allowing product to be shipped nationwide and installed with reduced on-site time.
teachings.quote: We literally have carts and we apply it and then we let it dry so we do a pre-finished floor but it's still an oxidation finish.

actionable_advice: Check wood moisture content before installation using NWFA regional moisture maps at nwfa.org and ensure readings match the target for your climate zone.
Sticker stack wood on-site prior to installation by alternating board directions to allow airflow through the entire stack.
Climatize the home with temporary HVAC set to the homeowner's intended living temperature for at least two weeks before installing wood floors.
Budget approximately $10,000 per HVAC unit for temporary climatization, as each five-ton unit costs $5,000-$6,000 plus filters and maintenance.
When covering finished floors during construction, use Masonite or denim board as a floating cover and never tape anything directly to the finished floor surface, as tape will pull the finish off.
Allow finished floors to cure without covering for at least two to three days before placing protection on top.
Ask wood floor installers to stop at natural transition points such as thresholds or hatboards when phasing installation across large homes.
Direct wood flooring installers needing to improve craftsmanship skills, especially for patterns, metals, and complex inlays, to attend National Wood Flooring Association (NWFA) training courses.
For radiant heat installations, install humidifiers to add moisture to the home and climatize the floor before activating the radiant heat system.

When installing metal brass inlay, ensure the floor is sanded flat first, use a router for a clean cut, get the metal flush with the floor surface, and be careful not to overheat the metal with a grinder as heat will cause it to rise.
For chevron floors, apply finish to the angled cuts before installation rather than adding pigment after installation to avoid bleeders at the cut joints.
Source metal inlays and brass thresholds through local metal suppliers and ask for lacquered, unlacquered, oil-rubbed, or stainless steel options depending on the design.
Stick to a maximum of three inches wide for solid hardwood planks before moving to engineered product to reduce movement and cupping risk.
Use bona for water-based finishes and WOCA for oil finishes as recommended brands.
client_challenges: A participant in North Carolina could not find a qualified local installer capable of doing brass metal inlay or two-tone border pattern work on high-end custom homes.
A participant had a chevron floor installed with visible discoloration bleed marks at all the angled cuts and did not know the cause or how to prevent it in future projects.
A participant asked how to blend and feather wood floor finishes across zones on large homes where the floor is installed or finished in multiple phases.
A participant had wood floors that had been stored in a house for six months and wanted to know if additional climatization was still needed before installation.
A participant in Austin asked whether engineered floors are truly better than solid hardwood for the Texas climate due to expansion and contraction.
A participant asked how to source and order custom hardwood pattern floors with intricate designs such as a Greek key border and had difficulty finding reputable suppliers.
A participant asked about radiant heat compatibility with wood floors and how to prevent gapping and damage.
A participant asked whether applying an oxidation finish on top of a pre-engineered pre-finished product is advisable.
A participant asked who is responsible for covering and protecting installed hardwood floors during the remainder of construction.
notable_quotes: On showing up: 'All you got to do is get up in the morning show up to work and be ready to answer those phone calls.'
On shoe mold: 'Shuma is to cover any imperfection of an installer, that's what it is.'
On the value of craftsmanship over shortcuts with oil finishes: 'Oil finish is so forgiving that if you mess it up you need to stop installing floors and start selling tacos.'
On not taping floor coverings to finished floors: 'It's floating, the whole house, it does not get taped to the floor, it'll pull the finish right off.'

On working through mistakes: 'It's not about the money, it's about hey let's get this done right... i have messed up a lot of floors over the last 20 years and i have taken my medicine and those are the guys who have saved my life.'
On encouraging a struggling installer: 'Tell your person that i know his pain but he just got to keep trying and he's going to get there.'
